title: Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction
subtitle: 58 y/o male, 10 minutes into a PRBC transfusion
nursesNote: 1410 - 58 y/o male, post-op day 1 after partial colectomy, ordered 1 unit of packed red blood cells (PRBCs) for symptomatic anemia (Hgb 6.8). Two-RN verification of client ID, blood type, and unit completed; transfusion started at 1400 via a Y-tubing filter set with 0.9% normal saline. At 1410 (about 10 minutes in) the client becomes anxious, reports chills, low back/flank pain, and a 'sense of doom.' Temperature has risen from 98.6F to 101.8F. He reports the infusion site 'burns.' Approximately 40 mL has infused.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Identify signs of a transfusion reaction
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) findings that BEST indicate an acute transfusion reaction.

opts.text: Chills and a temperature rise to 101.8F within 10 minutes of starting the unit
opts.rat: Fever and chills occurring soon after starting a transfusion are hallmark early signs of a transfusion reaction and require immediate attention.

opts.text: Low back/flank pain and burning at the infusion site
opts.rat: Flank/back pain and infusion-site burning are classic signs of acute hemolytic reaction from red-cell destruction.

opts.text: New hypotension (90/52) with tachycardia (HR 120)
opts.rat: A sudden drop in blood pressure with tachycardia signals a severe systemic reaction and hemodynamic compromise.

opts.text: Anxiety and a reported 'sense of doom'
opts.rat: A sense of impending doom and acute anxiety frequently accompany an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction.

opts.text: Pre-transfusion hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL
opts.rat: The low hemoglobin is the reason for the transfusion, not a sign of a reaction.

opts.text: Baseline blood pressure 126/74 at 1400
opts.rat: This is a normal pre-transfusion baseline, not a reaction cue.

opts.text: Two-RN verification was completed before starting
opts.rat: Correct verification is a safety step, not a clinical sign of a reaction.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Link findings to the type of reaction
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret these findings.

opts.text: Fever, flank pain, hypotension, and burning at the site together suggest an acute HEMOLYTIC reaction
opts.rat: This cluster—fever, flank/back pain, hypotension, and infusion-site pain—is characteristic of acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, usually from ABO incompatibility.

opts.text: Antibody-mediated destruction of transfused red cells can lead to hemoglobinuria and acute kidney injury
opts.rat: Hemolysis releases free hemoglobin that can turn urine dark and damage the kidneys; severe cases can trigger DIC.

opts.text: The reaction is severe and requires the transfusion to be stopped immediately
opts.rat: Signs of hemolysis with hemodynamic instability are an emergency; the transfusion must be stopped at once.

opts.text: These findings most likely represent a mild allergic (urticarial) reaction that needs only an antihistamine
opts.rat: A mild allergic reaction causes hives/itching without fever, flank pain, or hypotension; this presentation is far more serious.

opts.text: The findings indicate simple circulatory overload (TACO) and should be treated with a diuretic while continuing the unit
opts.rat: TACO causes fluid overload (hypertension, crackles, dyspnea), not fever with flank pain and hypotension; continuing the unit would be dangerous here.

opts.text: The client is simply anxious and the transfusion can continue at a slower rate
opts.rat: Attributing hemolytic signs to anxiety and continuing the transfusion could be fatal.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO incompatibility)
opts.rat: Fever, flank pain, hypotension, site burning, and doom within minutes make acute hemolytic reaction most likely.

opts.text: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction
opts.rat: Common and causes fever/chills, but does not typically cause flank pain, hypotension, and infusion-site burning.

opts.text: Allergic (urticarial) reaction
opts.rat: Causes hives/itching; would not explain fever with flank pain and hypotension.

opts.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)
opts.rat: Causes dyspnea, hypertension, and crackles from volume overload—not this hemolytic picture, and only 40 mL has infused.

opts.text: Anxiety unrelated to the transfusion
opts.rat: Least likely: the objective fever, hypotension, and flank pain point to a true reaction, not anxiety alone.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate immediate interventions.

opts.text: STOP the transfusion immediately
opts.rat: Stopping the transfusion at the first sign of a serious reaction is the single most important action to limit hemolysis.

opts.text: Keep the IV line open with 0.9% normal saline using NEW tubing (not the blood set)
opts.rat: Maintaining IV access with normal saline through new tubing preserves the line for treatment and supports blood pressure/renal perfusion without infusing more blood.

opts.text: Notify the provider and the blood bank
opts.rat: The provider directs treatment and the blood bank must investigate the reaction and re-check compatibility.

opts.text: Stay with the client, monitor vital signs closely, and reassess frequently
opts.rat: Close monitoring detects worsening hemodynamics or airway compromise so care can be escalated quickly.

opts.text: Slow the transfusion and continue observing the client
opts.rat: The transfusion must be stopped, not slowed; continuing to infuse incompatible blood worsens hemolysis.

opts.text: Flush the remaining blood in the tubing into the client to avoid waste
opts.rat: Flushing residual blood into the client delivers more incompatible cells and is dangerous.

opts.text: Discard the blood bag and tubing in the trash
opts.rat: The blood bag and tubing must be saved and returned to the blood bank for investigation, not discarded.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Stop the transfusion
opts.rat: FIRST—immediately halting the infusion stops delivery of incompatible blood.

opts.text: Disconnect the blood tubing and keep the line open with 0.9% normal saline via new tubing
opts.rat: SECOND—maintains IV access and supports perfusion without infusing more blood.

opts.text: Assess vital signs and airway, and stay with the client
opts.rat: THIRD—rapid reassessment guides how urgently care must be escalated.

opts.text: Notify the provider and the blood bank
opts.rat: FOURTH—the provider orders treatment and the blood bank begins the reaction workup.

opts.text: Send the blood bag, tubing, and required blood/urine specimens to the blood bank/lab
opts.rat: LAST of these steps—returning the unit and specimens allows compatibility re-testing and hemolysis workup.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After initial management, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: Blood pressure rises to 118/70 after normal saline and the chills resolve
opts.rat: Recovering blood pressure and resolving symptoms indicate the client is stabilizing after the transfusion was stopped.

opts.text: Urine output is clear yellow and adequate (about 40 mL/hr)
opts.rat: Clear, adequate urine suggests the kidneys are being protected from hemoglobin-related injury.

opts.text: Client passes dark, tea-colored (red-brown) urine
opts.rat: Dark urine reflects hemoglobinuria from ongoing hemolysis and a risk of acute kidney injury—escalate care.

opts.text: Blood pressure continues to fall to 78/40 despite normal saline
opts.rat: Persistent, worsening hypotension signals a severe reaction requiring emergency intervention.

opts.text: Temperature returns to 98.8F and the client reports feeling calm
opts.rat: Resolving fever and anxiety indicate the reaction is settling after prompt management.

opts.text: New oozing of blood from the IV site and gums appears
opts.rat: New bleeding from multiple sites suggests disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), a serious complication of hemolytic reaction.

opts.text: Heart rate remains 118 with no change after the first liter of saline
opts.rat: A persistently elevated heart rate that has not changed reflects ongoing instability rather than improvement.

opts.text: Oxygen saturation stays at 93% on room air
opts.rat: An unchanged, borderline saturation shows the client has neither improved nor deteriorated on this measure and needs continued monitoring.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
